data.title: Copart: Lower In-Yard Times Will Improve Profitability (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
In my last coverage of Copart, Inc. (NASDAQ:CPRT), I downgraded its rating due to valuation concerns, since its stock was trading above fair value, even when assuming healthy revenue growth and a margin expansion.

Since then, my expectations about the performance of the business have not changed, but the valuation has come down to a reasonable level, providing investors with the opportunity to buy this outstanding business at a fair price.

The Investment Thesis

Copart has a market cap of $46B and is the industry leader in online vehicle auctions (~40% market share). Since most of its revenues are generated from transaction fees, the company serves as a natural hedge against inflation.

Its VB3 auction platform provides a significant competitive advantage due to network effects, since buyers (dismantlers, mechanics, and resellers) will go to the marketplace with the most listings, and sellers (mainly insurance companies) want to list on the platform with the most traffic.

Although a marketplace already provides a significant competitive advantage, Copart's moat is even stronger, since it also owns the yards to store the vehicles on behalf of insurance companies.

For someone to compete with Copart, it wouldn't be enough to build a marketplace, since it would take decades to build the yard network, and arguably, it wouldn't be possible given that the number of yards close to cities is limited.

On top of the strong competitive advantages, Copart has a highly profitable business model run by an experienced (and well-incentivized) management team, which has resulted in above-average returns.

Lower In-Yard Times Will Improve Profitability

At the beginning of the article, I mentioned that my expectations about the performance of the business have not changed. Well, that is not completely true, since my expectations about Copart's profitability have increased. Here is why.

Since 1994, Copart has grown from 15 locations to over 200 yards across 11 countries, and the company is now leveraging the international expansion to keep up with the growth rates.

Revenues are expected to grow at a healthy ~10% CAGR over the next five years, but as Copart becomes larger, the opportunities to expand its footprint will diminish, as reflected in the reinvestment rate and the increasing cash balance (9.5% of Copart's market cap).

While a lower reinvestment rate is not positive news, it could be compensated for with higher free cash flow margins.

Once the network to process salvage vehicles is built, less capital needs to be deployed into land acquisitions, and D&A decreases as a percentage of revenues, leading to higher margins.

As reflected in the chart above, Copart enjoys significant operating leverage, since an additional transaction on its platform has declining marginal costs.

Copart has always been at the forefront of innovation, and the company is now focused on reducing cycle times by leveraging AI to reduce manual interactions in the auto loss process.

As a result of its partnership with Hi Marley in late 2023, Copart launched the AI tools Total Loss Express 360, Rapid Total Loss, and Title Express in 2024, which are helping to reduce inventory levels and in-yard times:

In addition, our continuous focus on reducing our operational cycle times has reduced inventory levels. For example, deploying our Title Express solution to a number of new carriers has reduced in-yard cycle times and physical inventory.

Source: Leah Stearns (CFO) - Q3 2025 Earnings Call.

Looking ahead, I expect Copart's margins to expand further, benefiting from operating leverage, improved efficiency, and higher inventory turnover.

What To Do With The Cash Pile?

As mentioned before, I don't have a positive view on the decrease of the reinvestment rate, since the combination of high reinvestment rates and high returns on invested capital is a perfect formula for outstanding shareholder value creation.

Nevertheless, on the positive side, fewer yard acquisitions free up capital that can be deployed elsewhere.

Since Copart has zero debt, this only leaves us with three alternatives to deploy the excess cash: generate interest income on the cash balance, buybacks, and business acquisitions.

At the moment, Copart is choosing the first option, since it has $4.4B invested in short-term U.S. Treasury Bills and money market accounts, which have produced an interest income of $172M over the last year (4.4% yield).

Given that Copart operates in a concentrated industry, it might close opportunistic acquisitions from time to time, but I don't expect a significant amount of cash to be deployed into acquisitions.

For reference, Copart has only closed three acquisitions over the last decade, deploying less than $300M in total. In 2017, it acquired an auction platform for sports vehicles (motorcycles, snowmobiles, and watercraft), a U.K. parts recycler in 2022, and a heavy equipment auction company in 2024 (Purple Wave).

The last option would be reducing the share count, which the company has done in the past, but not at the current multiples.

Since 2017, Copart has not deployed meaningful amounts of cash in stock buybacks, which is consistent with the valuation multiple expansion.

If the current cash balance were spent on buybacks, the share count could decrease by 10%, boosting earnings per share, but the capital allocation discipline in relation to the price paid on share buybacks is positive, especially in the current interest rate environment.

The cash balance is producing an interest income of $172M per year ($0.18 per share), which represents a 10.1% increase in operating income. In addition, the huge cash pile provides optionality to take advantage when opportunities to acquire land or other businesses at attractive prices arise.

Although Copart doesn't consistently repurchase shares, historically, it has drastically reduced the share count when the cash pile was too high and valuation was between 20x and 25x net income, like it did in FY 2016, when it reduced the share count by 9.1%.

Considering Copart's approach to stock buybacks, I don't rule out an aggressive buyback if the stock declines below $40 per share.

Copart's Fair Value

To determine Copart's fair value, I use a DCF approach with an 8% discount rate (4.2% risk-free rate, 3.5% equity-risk premium, and 1.04 beta).

As shown in the table below, I expect revenues to grow at high single digits, albeit declining over time as expansion opportunities diminish.

Depending on the source consulted, the industry is expected to grow between 4% and 13.7% CAGR in the U.S., and by 17.4% CAGR internationally until 2030, consistent with the analyst consensus estimates.

In my view, considering Copart's competitive position, the ongoing international expansion, and the benefits from inflation (variable fees are calculated as a % of the auction price), revenues growing between 8% and 9% over the next years is a realistic assumption.

On one hand, the development of autonomous vehicles should reduce accidents over time, acting as a headwind for Copart's revenues. However, on the other hand, higher repair costs due to increasing complexity, more miles driven per vehicle, and the increased vehicles on the roads will support demand for Copart's services.

Based on the presented assumption, which considers a decrease in D&A as a percentage of Copart's revenues and a steady margin expansion, Copart's stock is fairly priced at $55 per share.

At the moment, the stock presents a 15% upside to its fair value, and assuming a 25x LTM FCF exit multiple (the 10-year average is 31x), it would deliver an 11.3% CAGR going forward.

Risks To The Thesis

Although the growth of electric vehicles is net positive for Copart, given that repair costs are higher, driving up total loss frequency ratios, the development of self-driving cars might not.

For now, the growth of autonomous vehicles is going slower than expected, and the high-interest-rate environment doesn't support the development of an industry that will require significant capital for years without yielding any short-term profits.

According to a recent S&P Global Mobility research, by 2034, autonomous networks, such as Alphabet's Waymo, will reach a market share of around 1.5% in the U.S., and 5% of sales of light vehicles in China will be autonomous.

Despite the slow development of autonomous vehicles (Tesla vehicles operate at a Level 2 automation), it is a risk worth considering for long-term investors, since autonomous vehicles will reduce accidents, and car ownership could decline with self-driving vehicle networks in big cities.

Repair costs of autonomous vehicles are higher due to the increased number of electronic components, chips, and sensors, which could compensate for fewer accidents, but we have yet to see the net impact on Copart's business.

Conclusion

Copart is delivering double-digit growth rates supported by the international expansion and higher total loss frequency rates due to increased vehicle complexity and repair costs.

Although I expect the reinvestment rate to decline further as the company becomes larger, it will be offset by the increase in profitability and lower in-yard times.

Copart's stock has declined by 20% from its ATH, and is now trading at a 15% discount to its fair value ($55 per share). Therefore, I am upgrading its rating from Hold to Buy.

In addition, Copart has a substantial cash balance that could support the stock price below $40 per share, where I expect buybacks to accelerate.

data.title: My 5% Rule: The Easiest Retirement Investing Strategy I'll Ever Share
data.description:
Introduction

December 12, 1980.

That's when Apple (AAPL) went public. Back then, it went public at $22.00 per share. Currently, it trades at roughly $230, indicating a gain of nearly 950%.

Interestingly enough, this translates to a CAGR of roughly 5.4%, which beats inflation. However, it's below the market's return of roughly 10% since then.

Now, bear in mind that I just committed a crime, as I completely ignored the company's many stock splits. See, even if investors, for some weird reason, were excluded from all stock splits, they still would have done rather well.

To give you an idea of just how impressive Apple's real performance was, here's the company's history of stock splits:

  • 4-1 on August 28, 2020.
  • 7-1 on June 9, 2014.
  • 2-1 on February 28, 2005.
  • 2-1 on June 21, 2000.
  • 2-1 on June 16, 1987.

Adjusted for these splits, the company went public at just $0.10.

Using the correct approach, investors since then would have generated a CAGR of nearly 19%, bringing the total gain to nearly 69,000%. Even better, if we include the dividend, we get a total return of more than 87,600%.

There are a number of reasons why I'm bringing this up. One of them is the fact that when I wrote the article "3 Super Popular Stocks I Wouldn't Touch With A 10-Foot Pole," which included Apple, quite some people brought up their impressive investments, including someone who bought it in 1987:

Apple was my first stock purchase in 1987. Just lucky. I was stationed in San Francisco at the time and just heard about the company from fellow naval officers.

Actually more than one person told me about their Apple investment, which is why I simply had to find out how much money someone could have made.

As you may have guessed from the first part of this article, that number is quite high. For example, a $5,000 investment made on January 2, 1990, would have turned into nearly $4.4 million, including reinvested dividends.

If you invest that money in 10-year government bonds, you can make 4.2% per year, generating close to $185,000 in annual passive income, which is $15,400 per month, $506 per day, and $21 per hour.

Essentially, a $5,000 investment back then would have resulted in the hourly salary of a McDonald's restaurant general manager for life, so to speak.

Also, to make this even more silly, as Apple pays $0.26 in quarterly dividends, everyone who bought the stock in the first two decades of being public generates a yield on cost of roughly 260%, as the average price was roughly $0.40. For the fictional $5,000 investment person, that's a great deal.

Another reason I am bringing all of this up is that these textbook investing scenarios not only help us achieve wealth beyond our wildest dreams if we stick to a great company and hold it through recessions, pandemics, elections, and whatnot, but also because they show that once we're old, the differences between us are even bigger.

I'm mentioning this in nearly every single retirement-focused article, as it is impossible to come up with a one-size-fits-all approach.

  • Some people have lived paycheck to paycheck their entire lives, leaving little to no room to build a nest egg for retirement. These people are dependent on Social Security and their ability to keep working on the side. By 2033, 10% of all people aged 75 and over are expected to work, with being able to buy extras and avoiding reducing savings being the biggest reasons for "forced" employment after retirement.

  • Health is a factor we sometimes cannot influence. Some get to enjoy decades of retirement, while others don't. I think we all know these cases.
  • Investors who were disciplined and capable of sticking to a plan (like our fictional $5,000 Apple investor) don't have to worry much about retirement. Odds are, they can afford a very lavish lifestyle and expensive care.
  • Some inherit millions, while others have to take care of family members, creating unexpected financial complications. Right now, Gen X is somewhat wedged between caring for their parents and supporting their children.

As we can see below, anyone currently making less than $90K in pre-retirement income is risking an income replacement rate of more than 80%, meaning they will need to make almost their entire income in retirement income to maintain their lifestyles. That's a tough nut to crack for many.

This is one of the reasons why many people start to invest way too early with a focus on income. It's a topic very close to my heart, as I have seen countless people blow up their portfolios owning some of the worst high-yielding stocks money can buy. In many cases, they ended up with both capital losses and dividend cuts, setting them back in their financial journey.

Especially for these people, falling behind is worse than for people with more capital. After all, if a millionaire investor buys a luxury watch that turns out to be worthless after a decade, it won't derail their financial plan.

A middle-class (or lower-class) investor investing his or her hard-earned cash in dividend investments is much more prone to setbacks, which can be costly.

That's what this article is all about, as I present my "5% rule," which is another very straightforward concept of mine that I believe can help many people plan for retirement and invest with a much lower risk of making bad mistakes.

So, let's get to it!

Don't Fall For The Yield Trap

Dividend investing is awesome. It really is.

As I wrote in an article that carried the title "Why Everyone Should Be A Dividend Investor," long-term investing in dividend (growth) stocks has benefits like long-term outperformance and subdued volatility, which essentially means we're getting higher returns at lower risks.

Although a dividend itself doesn't add value, because it simply shifts cash from a company's balance sheet to an investor's account, the fact that a company is able to pay and grow a dividend explains its attractiveness:

  • Dividend growers often have strong balance sheets, consistent earnings growth, healthy free cash flow growth, and business models that allow them to distribute cash to shareholders each year. After all, companies need to be in great financial shape to distribute dividends. Companies in bad shape are unable to consistently grow dividends.
  • Investors often prefer stability. They know they can trust a consistent dividend grower. While some high-risk/fast-growing companies may do better during extreme bull markets, dividend growers are much more consistent. Investors tend to prefer that.
  • If a company is consistently growing its dividend, it's fair to assume it has found a business model that has an edge over other companies. This bodes well for its future.
  • Dividends are income. If a stock you hold pays income, it's often easy to just hold these shares and add on weakness. This is one of the reasons why volatility tends to be low. If your stocks do not provide income, you'll have to gradually sell shares or use option strategies to generate a cash flow from your investment.
  • Dividends are often reinvested, which supports the stock price.

One mistake many investors make is assuming that if dividend investing is about buying companies that pay a dividend, we should aim for the highest dividend. To some degree, that sounds very reasonable.

The problem is that there are limits. Usually, the higher the yield, the higher the financial risk, as a company can only determine its dividend payout, and the market determines its yield.

That's why Apple is still not a high-yield company, as the market has caused the yield to remain low through elevated capital gains for many decades.

This is how Morningstar's David Harrell put it:

This is really the danger of buying stocks based on one metric or one number. You’re looking at stock, and stock A is yielding 3.5%, and stock B is yielding 6%. You’re like, “Oh, well, I’m going to buy stock B. It must be a better purchase.” And it could be. But sometimes there are what you refer to as dividend traps, and this is a situation where if we think about how yield is calculated—it’s a very simple calculation, it’s the current dividend rate of the stock divided by the share price—but if that share price starts to crater, to go down because the denominator is getting smaller and smaller, the resulting yield is going to be higher and higher. - Morningstar

Moreover:

If you see a high-yielding stock, it could just be a great company that’s paying out a lot of its earnings as a dividend, and it could be a good investment. It could also be a company that has seen its yield being pushed up by a declining share price. - Morningstar

For example, the Global X SuperDividend ETF (SDIV), which focuses on "100 of the highest dividend-paying equities around the world," yields 10%.

Even including dividends, it has lost 12% over the past ten years.

While there are some stocks with yields close to (or even above) 10% that I trust, in general, it's a recipe for disaster, and a haven of false hope for anyone forced to squeeze the most income out of their capital for retirement purposes.

The 5% Rule - Simple Yet Effective

The first time I brought up the 5% Rule was in an article on July 11, when I wrote the following:

I have the 5% strategy, which is as simple as it is effective.

[...]

That's why I started telling people, "Just imagine if you got 5% on your portfolio, how much would that be?" That's also why I stopped tracking my monthly dividend income.

While I am fully aware of where my dividends come from, what the yields are, and what growth rates I can expect, I stopped tracking my income performance. After all, as long as I am investing with a focus on dividend growth, the total yield simply does not matter.

Essentially, I am making the case that every investor who's not yet retired should simply focus on buying high-quality dividend growers without being obsessed with yield. Focus on growing your principal using proven dividend growth strategies.

Ideally, you will end up with a very high yield on cost in the future or enough principal capital to rotate it into new, higher-yielding stocks, and generate a safe 5% income to either fund an early retirement or generate enough additional income on top of Social Security to enjoy a great retirement.

As I explained in the article, we can gradually move money from the "Growth Citadel" to the "Bedrock Bastion" segment in the retirement pyramid (below).

Currently, I have an average portfolio yield of roughly 1.9%. While I have no goals of retiring anytime soon (or ever, really), this wouldn't cut it for me.

I have always had the 5% Rule in my head.

The moment I can cover my expenses with a 5% yield, I know I'm set, which makes the current yield of my portfolio nearly irrelevant.

By using this approach, I prevent sacrificing growth to buy income. While I am buying income stocks, I only buy them when I expect an elevated total return. This includes stocks like CME Group (CME) and Antero Midstream (AM).

My main goal is to build a dividend growth portfolio that comes with:

  • Wide-moat business model companies.
  • A lot of secular growth tailwinds.
  • Consistent dividend growth and strong free cash flow generation.
  • Safe balance sheets to reduce financial risks.

Meanwhile, I am also building a watchlist of my favorite higher-yielding stocks, as I want to be prepared to build strong passive income, and because I decided a while ago that I will give retired readers of mine a lot more attention.

Most of these picks fit my 5% Rule.

So, what really is the 5% Rule, you may ask?

For starters, if I had to summarize this strategy, it would be something like this: I am convinced that 5% is the sweet spot between buying income and dividend growth without taking on the risks of buying a "sucker yield."

Or to put it differently, I believe I can build fantastic dividend income portfolios picking from stocks yielding close to 5%.

This claim is based on a number of factors, including the interest rate on the government's 10-year bond, which has been hovering close to 4.2% for more than 24 months.

The higher the rate on government debt, the higher the income most investors require from higher-yielding stocks. After all, if I can get 4% from the government, a low-growth dividend income stock needs to provide a premium to justify taking stock market and company-specific risks.

For example, Realty Income (O), which is a low-growth net lease REIT with very dependable (monthly) income and Dividend Aristocrat status, has seen a slow but steady increase in its dividend yield since the bottom in bond yields.

Because Realty Income is almost a proxy for bonds due to its income/growth profile, it has seen pressure on its valuation since 2021, which makes sense.

There's even something called the Fed Model, which is based on the difference between the market's earnings yield (the inverse P/E ratio) and the nominal yield on the 10-year government bond.

According to the CFA Institute blog, "The intuition is that stocks and bonds are competing assets; therefore, buying riskier stocks only makes sense when stocks can out-earn risk-free US treasuries."

The beauty of the 5% Rule is that it allows us to build countless portfolios, with an average yield of roughly 100 to 150 basis points above the 10Y.

For example, we can play around with income, growth, and safety:

  • Let's say we buy one stock with an 11.0% yield. Blue Owl Capital Corp. (OBDC) comes to mind, which is a Business Development Company that lends to mid-market companies. It has a five-year CAGR of 3.6%. The biggest risk facing investors is credit stability risk, as its entire business model is based on finding a good balance between high-yield lending and finding safe portfolio companies.

  • We make Realty Income the backbone, with a 5.5% yield, Dividend Aristocrat status, and thousands of high-quality single-tenant assets leased by e-commerce-resistant tenants. It's boring, but that makes it such a great pick. Its yield comes with a five-year CAGR of 3.6%.

  • Because of this strong foundation, we can also add Carlisle Companies (CSL), which is one of my all-time favorite dividend growers with a yield of 1.2%, a payout ratio of a mere 20%, and a five-year CAGR of 15.0%. It has grown its dividend for nearly 50 consecutive years and has a large footprint in the fast-growing commercial building remodeling industry.

Although this is just a thought experiment, the combination of these three stocks provides an average yield of 5.8% if we give Realty Income a 50% weighting and the two yield outliers a 25% weighting, each. The average dividend growth rate is similar to that number, as CSL may have a low yield but higher dividend growth. Meanwhile, OBDC has a high yield but lower growth. Realty Income has the most safety and a yield that already fits the 5% Rule.

While this is just a fun exercise and not a serious 3-stock portfolio, it shows how we can play around with stocks and build income that perfectly fits our personal preferences without taking on elevated risks.

Another example supporting my 5% Rule is that the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) yields about 4.0%. It contains some of the market's top dividend stocks, which are known for the quality they offer.

Given that we can easily buy high-quality dividend stocks at a 4% yield, it doesn't take much to bump that yield to at least 5% by inclusion of higher-yielding stocks like Blue Owl Capital Corp, Realty Income, NNN REIT, Rexford Industrial, Altria (MO), MPLX (MPLX), Enbridge (ENB), covered-call option ETFs like Amplify CWP Enhanced Dividend Income ETF (DIVO), and so many others.

Moreover, the 5% Rule is adaptable to diverse financial goals and life stages, making it a versatile tool for retirement planning. For younger investors, it encourages accumulating shares in high-quality dividend growers with lower current yields but strong growth potential, like Carlisle Companies, which can compound into significant wealth over time.

Meanwhile, for those nearing or in retirement, the rule provides a clear target for transitioning into higher-yielding, stable assets like REITs, midstream companies, consumer staples, and others, providing sufficient income without excessive risk.

For example, a $100,000 investment with a 5% yield is an additional $5,000 worth of income. A $500,000 portfolio generates at least $25,000 in annual income following this rule.

Even better, because we can still incorporate growth, odds are the portfolio keeps its purchasing power and generates total returns that can keep up with the market.

The best thing is that, if needed, the strategy can also be combined with the 4% Rule, which is a rather well-known strategy I have covered before that says you can withdraw 4% of your nest egg annually, adjusted for inflation, with a high chance of not running dry over 30 years.

As we can see below, managing a stock-focused portfolio with a 4% withdrawal rate makes it very likely that capital will last at least 35 years in retirement, especially if we consider that stocks with an average yield of 5% tend to come with decent dividend growth and inflation protection.

And, on top of that, having a solid base of stocks that average 5% to 6% income allows us to make gradual adjustments. If investors suddenly require a higher income without a willingness to sell stock (4% Rule), they can gradually add higher-yielding stocks or transition certain positions to improve income without suddenly going all-in on risky investments.

After all, in asset management, making gradual adjustments is often the way to go. A strategy that works rarely (if ever) requires major adjustments.

Moreover, the only reason I didn't bring up more picks is that the main focus here is on my strategy. Needless to say, I'll provide a lot more actionable ideas in my daily articles going forward.

Takeaway

Apple's stock history is a masterclass in patience. In fact, it's a textbook example of what happens when one buys and holds high-quality stocks for a very long time.

As we discussed in the introduction, a $5,000 investment in 1990 would have ballooned to $4.4 million today.

Unfortunately, I have seen that many won't stumble onto the next Apple, as they simply chase yield, panic in downturns, and often hurt their net worth by buying sucker yields, believing dividend investing is all about buying income.

That's why I stick to my 5% Rule, which finds a sweet spot between growth and income, balancing safety and yield.

Even better, it's simple, as it builds a foundation of high-quality dividend growers early, then shifts toward steady 5% income later. There are no yield traps, no desperation moves, just a focus on quality compounding and transitioning to a yield that comes with a favorable risk/reward.

Hence, there are two major questions I care about:

  • What dividend growth companies can help me build long-term wealth?
  • How much capital do I need to retire on 5%?

It sounds simple, but I think it's one of the best approaches to building both wealth and income.

data.title: Dollar Slumps On The Anniversary Of The End Of Bretton Woods
data.description:
Overview

Today marks the 54th anniversary of the end of the Bretton Woods agreement that pegged the dollar to gold and other currencies to the dollar. Nixon, who was regarded as among the most conservative presidents of his generation, also announced a 90-day wage and price freeze and a 10% surcharge on imports. After some fits and starts, the modern era of floating exchange rates was introduced. Europe wanted little currency movement within its growing trade bloc, and after several experiments failed, it opted for a single currency in the late 1990s. The incredible asymmetry of power at the end of WWII made Bretton Woods possible in the first place. The US appears to no longer have the will or power to impose a new Bretton Woods but is too strong to allow others who are too divided to create a new order.

One of the early advocates of the “Mar-a-Lago accord," Stephen Morin, will soon be a governor on the Federal Reserve. This was supposed to be a mini-Plaza Agreement (September 1985 coordinated and repeated intervention to drive the dollar lower), but what US sees as a success, Japan cringes and China sees as a worrisome sign of America's strategy, and may have influenced its efforts to have the yuan shadow the dollar.

The greenback is trading with a softer profile today as yesterday's gains are pared. Stronger-than-expected Japanese GDP has lifted the yen, though the odds of a BOJ hike are slightly less than the swaps market had at the end of last month. As is often the case in a soft US dollar environment, the Canadian dollar is the laggard. It is jostling with sterling for the bottom of the G10 currencies today with about a 0.15% gain. Central European currencies and the Mexican peso are leading the emerging market currency complex today. East Asian currencies and the Turkish lira are the weakest. Despite disappointing Chinese economic data, the onshore yuan is trading slightly firmer. The large bourses in the Asia-Pacific and Europe have risen today. The notable exception is Hong Kong, and the index of mainland shares that trade there. Bond markets are under modest pressure. The 10-year JGB rose a couple basis points, while European yields are mostly around 2-4 bp higher. News that S&P upgraded India's sovereign credit to BBB from BBB- seemed to have little impact on Indian rates. It is the rating agency's first upgrade of India since 2007 and is now one notch ahead of Fitch and Moody's. The 10-year US Treasury yield is flat, near 4.285%. Gold is consolidating at the lower end of yesterday's range, leaving it off around 1.7% this week, which, if sustained, would be the largest weekly loss since the end of June. September WTI extended yesterday's recovery slightly (to $64.15) before reversing and is now below $63.50. The oil market, in particular, may be sensitive to the outcome of President Trump and Putin's meeting in Alaska today. A joint press conference is expected afterward.

USD: After holding above Wednesday's low yesterday, the Dollar Index was trading firmer ahead of yesterday's stronger-than-expected PPI. It was bid through Wednesday's high (almost 98.15) to meet the (38.2%) of the losses since Monday's high (~99.30). It is trading softer today but inside yesterday's range. It probably takes a move above 98.50-65 or a break of 97.30 to signify anything of technical importance. There is a slew of US data today: July retail sales, industrial production, import/export prices, business inventories, the August Empire State manufacturing survey, and the preliminary August University of Michigan consumer confidence and inflation expectation survey. Strong auto sales look to have flattered retail sales, but even the measure that excludes autos, gasoline, food services, and building materials appear to have held up. Still, anecdotal reports warn of downside risk. Industrial production and manufacturing output is expected to be flat after a 0.3% and 0.1% increase in June, respectively. Import prices may have risen by 0.1% for the second consecutive month. If so, the cumulative change this year would be flat. Since import prices do not include the tariffs, it would suggest that overall foreign producers are absorbing the tax increase, even if there are reports of individual companies or industries that might be accepting narrower profit margins into order to maintain market share. The University of Michigan's inflation expectations are expected to remain elevated. Lastly, the June Treasury's International Capital report is due late today. Despite the cries of capital flight of the tariffs or large deficit, the fact of the matter is that the net capital inflows in the first five months of the year (~$682 bln) are more than the first five months of 2023 (~$309 bln) and 2024 (~$95 bln) put together.

Euro: The euro fell by about 0.50% yesterday, its biggest loss since the end of July. At the same time, the US two-year premium over Germany widened for the first time in five sessions. The euro found support yesterday near the 20-day moving average (~$1.1630) and has held above $1.1645 today. The euro is firm in the European morning, probing the $1.1685-90 area. This week's low was set Monday close to $1.1590. Below there, initial support is seen near $1.1560, but a break of $1.1520 would undermine the technical tone. The week's high was set on Wednesday around $1.1730.

CNY: After falling to its lowest level since July 28 yesterday (~CNH7.1680), the dollar rebounded to a new session high (~CNH7.1830) around midday in NY. It reached nearly CNH7.19 today but is now back to around CNH7.1835. We note that after being inversely correlated with the dollar-yen in April and again in May, the 30-day rolling correlation has been above 0.60 since mid-July. The dollar's recovery against the yen yesterday seemed to favor the high dollar fix today from the PBOC. After it set the dollar's reference rate at its lowest level since last November (CNY7.1337) yesterday, the PBOC set the fix today at CNY7.1371 today. China reported softer real sector data. Year-over-year retail sales slowed to 3.7% (from 4.8%) and were the slowest in five months. Poor weather (hot, rains, and floods), coupled with the government's efforts to rein in excess capacity ("anti-involution" campaign), appeared to have slowed industrial production to 5.7% year-over-year rate from 6.8% in June. The surveyed jobless rate ticked up to 5.2% from 5.0%. The real estate market remains troubled. New and used house prices continue to fall, and property investment has yet to stabilize. Reports suggest Beijing is considering "asking" state-owned enterprises to buy homes. Earlier this week, China announced plans to subsidize part of the interest payments on some consumer loans.

JPY: If Treasury Secretary Bessent's call on the BOJ to hike rates weighed on the greenback against the yen initially yesterday, the jump in US rates following the PPI reported overwhelmed it. The greenback recovered from JPY146.20 to almost JPY148.00. However, stronger-than-expected Japanese growth pushed the dollar back down. It is testing the JPY146.75-80 area in the European morning. Japan's economy grew by 1.0% at an annualized rate in Q2, compared with a 0.4% expansion projected by the median forecast in Bloomberg's survey. The 0.2% contraction in Q1 was revised away. The economy is now said to have expanded by 0.6% in Q1, making it the strongest G10 economy in Q1. The deflator moderated to 3.0% from 3.3%, which was also unexpected. Consumption increased by 0.2%, matching the revised performance in Q1. Business spending improved to 1.3% from 1.0%, and inventories that added 0.6% to GDP in Q1 subtracted 0.3% from Q2 growth. Net exports, which were a 0.8% drag in Q1, were net contributors in Q2 (0.3%). Since the beginning of July, the swaps market has ranged from pricing in 10 bp of tightening this year to a little more than 20 bp. It is a little below 17 bp to end the week, up from about 14.5 bp at the end of last week after finishing July slightly below 18 bp.

GBP: On the back of the stronger-than-expected Q2 GDP yesterday, sterling reached almost $1.36, its best level since July 10. However, the broader dollar recovery after the US PPI sent sterling lower. It fell to about $1.3520, meeting the (38.2%) retracement of this week's rally. There are options for about GBP555 mln at $1.3520 that expire today. Yesterday's low has held today, and sterling is trading firmly in the $1.3560 area. A band of resistance is seen in the $1.3600-30 area. Since the August 1 low, sterling has rallied more than three cents (~3.5%), and it looks stretched in the short run. To sustain the momentum, sterling must settle above $1.3520.

CAD: The US dollar posted a bullish outside up day against the Canadian dollar by trading on both sides of Wednesday's range and settling above its high. In fact, greenback posted its highest settlement this month and nearly reached the (61.8%) retracement of the loss since the August high found around CAD1.3820. However, the US dollar stalled there and has been pushed back below CAD1.3800. The CAD1.3720-40 area looks like solid support now. Existing home sales rose 3.8% in July (2.8% in June). The June manufacturing and wholesale sales, due today, are not typically market movers.

AUD: The Australian dollar initially rose above Wednesday's high yesterday, encouraged by a firm employment report. It approached $0.6570, the best level since July 28. However, as the greenback caught a bid after the PPI, the Aussie reversed and was sold through Wednesday's low (~$0.6515), before finding support slightly in front of Tuesday's low ($0.6480). The Aussie settled below $0.6500 for the first time since August 5. The loss allowed the Australian dollar to nearly meet the (61.8%) retracement of this month's rally, which began on August 1. In line with the broadly weaker greenback, the Aussie is near session highs (~$0.6515) in late European morning turnover. There are options for a little more than A$560 mln at $0.6523 that expire today (and nearly A$675 mln struck at $0.6515 that expire early next week).

MXN: The combination of the US dollar broad rally, the jump in US rates, and the sell-off of most emerging market currencies took a toll on the Mexican peso. The greenback seemed poised to move higher after it bounced after setting a marginal new low for the year near MXN18.51 on Wednesday. It reached around MXN18.8530 yesterday before stabilizing. It was the largest dollar gain in nearly three weeks. The greenback has been sold into the bounce and is trading around MXN18.73-75 now. A close below the MXN18.68 area would neutralize the constructive US dollar's technical tone. The Brazilian real's price action was similar, even if less dramatic. The US dollar was sold to a new low for the year on Wednesday (~BRL5.38) but settled a little above Tuesday's close. Dollar buying yesterday lifted it to almost BRL5.4250. This week's high, set Monday, was near BRL5.46.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Altria: The Odds Of This Dividend King Returning To Its Old Glory?
data.description:
Introduction

Despite macro uncertainty, continued traditional cigarette declines, and regulatory risks, Altria (NYSE:MO) has been one of the best performing stocks in my portfolio, and in the market over the past year.

While many investors considered the Dividend King a "has been" due to increased risks, the company seems to be returning to its old glory with their strong price appreciation.

Moreover, seeing potential tailwinds from declining interest rates and an expected dividend increase in the near future, Altria could be poised to see more upside.

In this article, I discuss their latest earnings, growth drivers, and why MO could see additional upside over the next year or two.

Previous Thesis

I last covered Altria after their Q1 earnings in an article titled: Why Altria Is Still Cheap With More Upside Left.

Despite MO's strong price appreciation, their transition away from traditional smoking products, low forward P/E, and robust cash flows supported my thesis on why I considered them to be still attractive with significant upside left.

Since, Altria's price appreciation has beaten the S&P's (SP500), up nearly 11% compared to the index's 9.24% over the same period.

Latest Quarter Showed Solid Growth

During Altria's Q2 earnings, the tobacco giant managed to beat analysts' estimates on both their top and bottom lines. Earnings per share were $1.44, a beat of $0.06, while net revenue amounted to $6.1 billion, a solid beat by $910 million.

Quarter-over-quarter EPS grew from $1.23. Net revenue declined modestly from $6.2 billion in Q2 2024. However, revenues net of excise taxes increased slightly from $5.277 billion to $5.290 billion. Year-over-year their bottom line growth was a solid 8.3%, driven by frequent share repurchases and higher adjusted operating income.

For the first half, EPS growth was a solid 7.2% from $2.49 to $2.67. However, in comparison to peer Phillip Morris (PM), this was significantly lower than the 17.7% growth the latter saw year-over-year.

Smokeable Products OCI margins were up 4.2% to $2.9 billion. Oral Tobacco OCI margins increased double-digits, driven by continued market share growth in on!. For the first half of 2025, adjusted OCI margins were 64.5%, up from 61.6% the previous year.

On! saw its retail share increase 0.7 points from the year prior, reaching a retail share of 8.7%. Overall oral tobacco industry volume continued to grow while on! shipment volumes were up a sizable 26.5% annually.

However, this still trails significantly behind peer British American Tobacco's (BTI) VELO, whose market volume share grew 350 basis points annually to 29.7%.

As a result of their solid performance, Altria's management increased full-year EPS guidance by $0.05 on the lower end. This is now expected to be between $5.35 - $5.45, representing a growth rate of approximately 4%.

But management also stated they expect earnings growth to moderate in the back half of the year due to less buybacks as well challenged consumers. Even so, it's likely management will deliver on seeing at least 4% growth for the. year.

Catalysts That Could Drive Further Upside

Although the stock is up double-digits in the last year, Altria's growth drivers and tailwinds from lower interest rates could drive further upside.

Regarding the lawsuit against JUUL to halt the sale of NJOY products, management completed the development of a revised NJOY ACE product that they expect will address previous patents.

Management wasn't able to narrow down a date, but I expect them to try to get this done expeditiously to get NJOY back on the market, and it to have a potentially positive impact on the company going forward.

There's also the joint venture with Japan-Tobacco (OTCPK:JAPAF) to launch Ploom and SWIC, a heated tobacco device to further shift away from traditional cigarettes.

Furthermore, on! continues to grow market share and be the company's biggest growth driver, while Marlboro continues to see its market share creep up. And on! PLUSis expected to hit the U.S. if the FDA approves the PTMA that was submitted last year.

If approved, that should result in accelerated growth in volumes and market share in a competitive market. Peer British American Tobacco (BTI) successfully launched VELO PLUS late last year in the U.S. and has reached 110,000 outlets so far.

Balance Sheet Progress

Altria continued to improve its balance sheet, managing to deleverage from 2.1x to their target of 2.0x. Altria had the lowest leverage ratio amongst its peers Phillip Morris and British American Tobacco, who both are targeting leverage ratios of 2.0x by the end of 2026.

Altria's liquidity position was strong with approximately $1.3 billion and $23.65 billion in long-term debt. Although this slightly increased quarter-over-quarter, this was lower from roughly $25 billion three years ago.

Can Altria Return To Glory?

Despite continued declines in traditional smoking, which continues to pose a risk, Altria's strategic shift towards a smokeless future has shown promise despite challenges.

And while I expect these to continue, their price target of nearly $78 by the end of 2026 suggests strong upside if the stock returns to its normal P/E.

Currently, this sits at 12.19x, still relatively cheap for a stock of MO's caliber. Moreover, with an expected dividend increase any day now, this could also help its case for further upside. Over the past decade, Altria's dividend has been sufficiently covered, thanks to their frequent share repurchases.

If Altria can successfully reach their price target of $77.87 by the end of next year this would put them near their 2017 price where they traded around $77 a share. In 2007 they traded around $85.

If they can successfully continue to grow on!'s market share, see a successful launch of on! PLUS and get NJOY back on the market, I think it's reasonable to believe they can see a price above $80 in the next 1 to 2 years. Especially if the U.S. continues their crackdown on illegal vapes.

Risks To Thesis

Obvious risks for Altria continue to be regulatory issues and traditional smoking declines. Although, traditional smoking declines did experience a lesser decline than last year.

In the first quarter, cigarette volume declines were 12% and 13% the previous year's quarter. You can see this from the graph below, which could be seen as good news.

Plus, Middleton Cigars actually saw an increase in shipment volumes of 3.7% during the quarter vs a decline of 0.9% in Q2'24.

However, declining volumes is something investors should continue to monitor. Moreover, the FDA's decision on the resubmission of NJOY ACE if disapproved could have a negative impact on the share price going forward as well.

Investor Takeaway

While Altria faces headwinds like changing consumer behaviors as a result of inflation, uncertainty surrounding the NJOY resubmission, and volume declines in traditional smoking, their earnings growth, growing OCI margins, and guidance raise is an indicator of their potential to see more upside.

Furthermore, strong growth in on!and a successful launch in on! PLUS could result in further margin expansion, which could also lead to additional upside over the next year or two. As a result of this, a near 6.2% dividend yield, and them likely to see tailwinds from lower interest rates, I continue to rate Altria a buy.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Phillips 66: The Trajectory Is Improving
data.description:
Shares of Phillips 66 (NYSE:PSX) have been a poor performer over the past year as the company has dealt with a difficult refining cycle and challenges in its chemicals unit. This has left shares down 10% from a year ago. Indeed, performance has been so disappointing that Elliott managed to win two board seats in its proxy fight. Given Elliott's success with streamlining Marathon Petroleum (MPC), I am optimistic this can be a constructive engagement. I last covered shares in May, rating PSX a "buy," and since then, the stock has returned 15%, broadly in-line with the market. With updated financials and several actions, now is a good time to revisit shares.

Phillips 66 has a highly-integrated model. As you can see below, it owns refineries, significant pipeline assets, a marketing business, retail fuel operations in Europe, and a chemical joint venture with Chevron (CVX). This gives it more diverse cash flow than a firm like Marathon or Valero (VLO), but it also has been a reason why activists see room to sell assets and narrow the company's focus.

Frankly, it appears that PSX is getting the message to an extent. In May, Phillips 66 agreed to sell a 65% stake in its European retail business, resulting in pre-tax cash proceeds of $1.6 billion. This will help focus the company's operations and support debt reduction. On the earnings call, management also said there are no "sacred cows" in terms of divesting assets. In the medium term, I question the benefits of housing the chemicals JV inside of PSX. However given how weak the chemical market is and how badly those stocks have performed, now is unlikely to be an opportune time to sell the asset, especially given it has a favorable cost structure in the Middle East.

Rather, I expect an ongoing source of speculation to be whether PSX decides to spin out its midstream assets into a separate MLP, especially given how MPC has done that to great effect with MPLX (MPLX). This is not my base case, but it is a definite possibility. Having rolled in DCP Midstream and PSXP, PSX has gone the other direction to more clearly consolidate control. Thanks to these actions, its midstream unit has grown significantly, and there will likely be an examination of the best structure going forward.

In the company's second quarter, we did see meaningful improvement. Phillips 66 earned $2.38, which beat estimates by $0.67. Adjusted EBITDA tripled sequentially to $2.5 billion, thanks to a stronger refining environment after a dismal Q1. While PSX owns multiple businesses, refining is generally the unit that causes the most QoQ volatility, and it is still the core of the business. Importantly, results here were strong.

PSX's facilities ran at 98% utilization in Q2, which was the best quarterly result in over 6 years. PSX has been engaged in a multiyear effort of turnaround and maintenance activity to upgrade the quality of its refineries after a period of underinvestment, and we are starting to see the benefits of this spending with better throughput. Plus, operating costs per barrel hit a four-year low of $5.46/barrel. Guidance on full year turnaround cost was reduced by $100 million to $400-$450 million, another incremental positive.

Overall, refining generated a $392 million pre-tax profit from a $936 million loss a year ago. Its realized refining margin was $11.25, up 12% from last year and by nearly $5 from Q1. Refiners make money on the spread between products and crude oil, known as a crack spread. Last year, crack spreads narrowed meaningfully amid strong global refinery throughput. With OPEC+ increasing oil production and global growth remaining positive, we have seen crack spreads rebound meaningfully. I view current levels as more in keeping with normal crack spreads with about ~$2-3 of upside relative to Q2 levels. With where spreads are now, we should see a similar or slightly higher margin in the third quarter.

Elsewhere, midstream EBITDA was $972 million, up from $885 million in Q1. It completed its Coastal Bend acquisition during the quarter. This alongside organic expansion activity should propel midstream towards $4.5 billion of EBITDA by 2027. Chemicals earned just $20 million. Tariff disruptions were particularly problematic, leading to excess supply in the quarter, as exports to China were diverted. Fortunately, it is seeing chemical capacity rationalization in Europe. Lower supply should help to stabilize pricing and profits, but I expect the recovery here to be gradual with results running below mid-cycle norms at least into 2027.

During the quarter, PSX generated $845 million in cash from operations, despite $1 billion of working capital drains. With this, it did $419 million of buybacks, and as a result of these repurchases, there has been a 4% share count reduction over the past year. Alongside this, PSX pays a 4% dividend yield, which I view as very secure. That is because management has structured its dividend and sustaining cap-ex needs to be covered by less-volatile midstream cash flows. However, its balance sheet is not where I would like to see it.

The company's debt to capital is now 41% whereas it wants net debt to capital below 30% by 2027. Given how cyclical and volatile refining margins can be, entities like PSX should not have much financial leverage. While it has $1.1 billion of cash on hand, it has $20.9 billion of debt. For comparison, management reiterated its aim for $17 billion of consolidated debt. Now, PSX added $2.4 billion of debt during the quarter to fund $2.2 billion of acquisitions in midstream.

This is why I was encouraged to see the $1.6 billion in proceeds from its European retail unit sale, and this will go to fund most of that acquisition and shifting from European retail to American midstream assets is a winning asset transition in my view. I do expect the company to continue to seek divestiture opportunities to speed up its deleveraging. Absent that, we are likely to see PSX slow the pace of repurchases to restore balance sheet strength.

Back in May, I was targeting $2.5-$3.25 billion in 2025 free cash flow and this is with chemicals and refining running below normal this year. I still see the company generating about $3 billion of free cash flow, assuming the current refining environment persists and working capital normalizes. With Elliott's involvement, I believe PSX is well positioned to hit its financial targets and approach $7 billion in free cash flow in 2027. I was also encouraged by operational improvement this quarter. As such, I still see shares gradually rising towards $165 over the next 18 months with asset sales a potential positive catalyst. I continue to see PSX as a buy.

data.title: Vertex Inc.: Moving To The Sidelines As Near-Term Growth Expectations Get Reset
data.description:
Investment summary

My previous investment thought on Vertex Inc. (NASDAQ:VERX) was an upgrade to a buy rating because the growth drivers were intact andthe valuation was also at a more reasonable level. However, 2Q25 results have tempered my conviction in the near term. While cloud momentum and early e-invoicing traction remain strong, the slowdown in ERP migrations and softer customer expansion activity have meaningfully reset expectations. With FY25 guidance cut and growth reacceleration now less certain in the near term, I think sentiment will stay muted until there’s tangible proof of recovery. As such, I am moving to the sidelines.

2Q25 Earnings Review

2Q25 results did not live up to my expectations. Headline numbers were solid. Revenue grew 14.6% y/y to $184.6 million, with software subscriptions up 15.7% and services up 8.3%. Gross margin also expanded >200 bps y/y to 75.9%. But this strength didn’t flow through to earnings. Adj. EBITDA was flat y/y at $38.4M, with margin compressing to 20.8% from 23.9%, which reflects heavier investment spending. Most notably, a cut to FY25 guidance overshadowed the positives this quarter showed, and I think this has led to the market resetting near-term expectations (share price fell 18% post 2Q25 earnings).

Cloud Growth and E-Invoicing

VERX cloud momentum remains the crux of my long-term bullish thesis. Performance here is clearly strong, with revenue growing by 29.9% y/y to $86.2 million, and management reaffirmed its FY25 cloud growth target of 28%. This speaks very well of the underlying demand because total revenue guidance was trimmed. For investors, I believe this matters because it basically shows that the weakness is in the on-premise business, which is cyclical noise, while the cloud business continues to move in the right direction. As a percentage of total subscription sales, cloud now accounts for 55%, up from 49% a year ago. From this, it is apparent that the mix shift is happening.

Apart from the cloud, e-invoicing, which I’ve previously highlighted as a major long-term optionality, is also showing its first tangible sign of materialization. I got this inkling from the fact that Ecosio's annual recurring revenue [ARR] grew 33% sequentially to $10.8 million. This is a massive acceleration, clearly showing that VERX is benefiting from the strong deal flow ahead of upcoming European e-invoicing mandates. As a recap, Belgium’s deadline is set for January 2026, and as France and Germany’s deadlines get cast in stone, I expect more companies to rush into getting the e-invoicing set up and done to avoid compliance issues.

This dynamic goes beyond just VERX benefiting from a strong flow of deals; it also significantly extends the growth runway as there is a land-and-expand motion going on. Management gave a good example here:an early customer has already expanded to additional country coverage post-launch. If this trend continues, it should become a key driver of sustained net revenue retention [NRR] expansion.

Macro Headwinds and Lowered Expectations Reset the Clock

All the forward-looking growth angles are great, but I see a need to downgrade this to hold rating for the near term because of a few reasons. Firstly, the risk of a needed 2H25 reacceleration that I mentioned in my risk section previously has unfortunately materialized, but not in the way I would have hoped. It is evident from the 2Q25 results that the business is indeed being impacted by macro headwinds. You can see this from organic ARR growth slowing to 14.1% y/y from 15.1% in 1Q25, and NRR declining one point sequentially to 108%.

What is more worrisome is that management said there was a slowdown in large ERP migrations, which is elongating deal cycles. I see this as a very negative development because the ERP upgrade cycle was not just a minor tailwind; I actually saw this as one of the most visible and strong drivers of ARR growth for the next three years. This slowdown directly challenged my bull thesis. My idea was that because tax compliance is mission-critical and must be embedded into the core financial stack, through this ERP migration, VERX gets pulled into the migration timeline early, giving it a valuable "head start" to cross-sell its broader platform. With ERP projects now being delayed, VERX loses this crucial, early entry point, and the downstream impact is a delay in VERX sales pipeline. This creates a narrative problem as it impacts investor confidence and expectations given that the high-visibility, multi-year $1.8 billion TAM opportunity tied to these migrations now appears less certain in the near term. While the CEO noted this cycle has been slow in 2025 but expects it to accelerate eventually, I don’t think this explanation is enough to help investors regain confidence.

Additionally, there was a pronounced slowdown in customers’ own business growth. This is also problematic because this means that customers are keeping their existing contracted usage bands. In other words, there is reduced incremental revenue from extra usage that VERX can benefit from.

All in all, this drove management to guide down FY25 revenue, now at $750–$754 million, which I think has really hurt the stock sentiment. In my view, this revision will likely keep the stock range-bound until there is tangible evidence of a recovery in expansion activity and a clear path to reacceleration.

Valuation

The foreseeable near-term is going to be tough for VERX. The stock has generally trended in line with its growth expectations. From late FY22 to early FY25, the market has continuously expected better growth in this period, and that has driven valuation (forward revenue) from ~4x all the way up to ~11.5x. However, with the headwinds I mentioned, especially the delay in ERP migration, growth expectations have reset very strongly. Consequently, valuation has come down. The way I see it, VERX has to show that growth can reaccelerate; if not, the stock will be unlikely to see any meaningful valuation re-rating. I downgrade to hold.

Conclusion

I downgrade VERX to a Hold rating. The near-term picture has changed for the worse, with macro headwinds and ERP migration delays impacting growth. Until VERX can demonstrate momentum in ARR growth and expansion activity, I expect the stock to remain range-bound. In my view, investors are going to wait for a clearer reacceleration before revisiting the stock.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: UnitedHealth Jumps As Funds Boost Stakes
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) takes new stakes in UnitedHealth (UNH), Nucor (NUE); pares back on Apple (AAPL). (00:22) Intel (INTC) spikes on speculation US considers taking stake in chip company: report. (01:22) Airbnb (ABNB) launches more flexible payment options with Reserve Now, Pay Later. (03:12)

This is an abridged transcript.

United Health is on the move this morning after 13F filings were disclosed.

Warren Buffett's Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) (NYSE:BRK.A) disclosed that it acquired a new stake in the health insurer during Q2 2025. It also took new stakes in Nucor (NUE) and D.R. Horton (DHI).

Berkshire acquired 5.04M shares of UnitedHealth (UNH) valued at $1.57B.

Its Apple (AAPL) holdings declined, as did its stake in Bank of America (BAC).

Eminence Capital exited its stake in Bank of America (NYSE:BAC), Uber (NYSE:UBER), GE Healthcare (NASDAQ:GEHC), Capital One (COF) and Primo Brands (PRMB), while taking new positions in UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH), Coupang (CPNG) and Peloton Interactive (NASDAQ:PTON), among other moves in the second quarter.

The global asset management firm disclosed new positions in UnitedHealth (NYSE:UNH) with 0.57M shares.

Renaissance Technologies is also betting on UnitedHealth Group (NYSE:UNH), taking a new stake of 1.35M shares.

Premarket United Health is up 13%.

Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) closed the day Thursday 7.38% higher after Bloomberg reported the Trump Administration is considering the U.S. taking a financial stake in the company.

The report said the plan would help Intel develop a factory hub in Ohio.

According to people familiar with the issue, At one point, Intel planned to develop the site into the largest chip foundry on the planet. There are no details on the amount the U.S. would invest in the company or even its legality as the plan remains "fluid."

"We look forward to continuing our work with the Trump administration to advance these shared priorities, but we are not going to comment on rumors or speculation," Intel said to Bloomberg.

The plan allegedly emerged from a meeting earlier this week between Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan and U.S. President Donald Trump at the White House.

Airbnb is now offering more flexible payment options.

U.S. guests can now reserve an Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) with no money down with the company’s “Reserve Now, Pay Later” option.

The new policy is available for listings with a moderate or flexible cancellation policy. It allows the guest up to the end of the listing’s free cancellation period to pay the full amount of the listing. Cancellation policies selected by hosts remain unchanged.

Airbnb (NASDAQ:ABNB) found that 60% of respondents to a recent survey said flexible payment options were important when making a reservation, while 55% said they use a flexible payment option when available. Additionally, 42% of respondents said they have delayed and missed out on their preferred accommodation option because of time spent coordinating payment with co-travelers.

The “Reserve Now, Pay Later” option is only available for U.S. guests booking eligible U.S.-based listings.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Applied Materials signals Q4 revenue decline to $6.7B amid China uncertainty and leading-edge logic delays

Cathie Wood bets big on Bullish IPO, buys over 2.5M shares

Oracle to offer Google’s Gemini AI models to its customers

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory after U.S. stocks finished Thursday's trading session mostly flat as hopes of progress on Russia and Ukraine countered a hit to sentiment from a significantly hotter than expected July PPI report. U.S. President Donald Trump will meet with Russian leader Vladimir Putin today in Alaska. By the way, that's the topic of today’s edition of the Wall Street Breakfast newsletter. Here’s a link to sign up.

Crude oil is down 0.9% at $61/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.4% at $118,000. Gold is up 0.25% at $3,344.

The FTSE 100 is flat and the DAX is up 0.4%. And the market in India is closed today for a holiday.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Applied Materials (NASDAQ:AMAT) -14% – Shares sank after the chip equipment maker topped FQ3 expectations but cut its Q4 outlook, citing macroeconomic and policy headwinds, including weaker demand from China.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales for the month of July. It will include the Amazon Prime Day event and corresponding sales events across the retail sector.

  • 9:15 am Industrial Production

data.title: Putting America First With First Solar
data.description:
I find it rather odd how climate change, environmental protection, and all of that dominate the discussion around solar panels. I believe humanity contributes to climate change, and I do believe in safeguarding the environment. Still, when I look at solar panels, the first thing I think of is “national security.” If the United States were to end up in a hot war with China, America’s energy infrastructure could quickly come under attack. While I find the prospect of a hot war with China low, as far as national security is concerned, we should plan for it as an inevitability, and among other things, that means strengthening our energy grid, and solar panels can contribute to that. Even outside of a hot war, cyber attacks by shadowy groups could knock out energy grids and energy infrastructure, including perhaps even oil pipelines and refineries.

(On a side note: there is some debate over how much solar panels will reduce humanity’s contribution to climate change, as producing the panels is energy-intensive, among other things. Additionally, solar panel production can damage the environment. Since I’m looking at panels from a national security POV, I am not as concerned about these factors, although important discussions should take place.)

Even outside of cyberattacks and wars, energy grids can be knocked out by storms and other events. Ultimately, strengthening and diversifying the energy grid could yield many benefits. Which is where First Solar (NASDAQ:FSLR) comes in. It is the largest producer of solar panels in the United States, producing 50% more panels than the next biggest American company. (The next biggest producer is Q-Cells, which is headquartered in South Korea but has production facilities in Georgia.)

There are many limitations to solar panels, of course. They only work in some climates and cloudy weather reduces energy production. They also only generate energy through part of the day. On its own, solar energy won’t be enough for most (if not all) energy grids and local demand markets. Still, they can reduce pressure on traditional energy grids, and in many cases, can provide energy even if the grid goes down, say due to a storm or cyberattack. That said, for grid-tied solar panels to provide energy when the grid goes down, they typically do need to be paired with a battery. Fortunately, battery storage costs have been declining over the years, making a battery setup more obtainable. Tariffs may send prices upward again, but still, historical trends suggest an overall decline.

Thesis

First Solar is an attractive investment opportunity with a fair valuation and good long-term prospects. President Donald Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill creates drawbacks, but also catalysts for growth, including protecting American solar manufacturers from dumping. China’s solar industry is a serious threat, but overcapacity and high debt burdens are pressuring Chinese firms.

Can America Challenge China’s Solar Domination?

China currently dominates the global solar market in all stages, which are illustrated above. China is also generating the most demand. Unfortunately, North America, and Europe as well, are relatively minor players. Other countries in the Asia Pacific (APAC) region have a larger presence in production, but are still relatively small players compared to China.

China’s efforts to dominate the solar panel industry have created an estimated 300,000 manufacturing jobs (ibid.). One might wonder if China has become overly reliant on manufacturing exports, as price wars and deflationary pressures mount, but regardless, it will continue to dominate the solar panel industry.

That said, First Solar offers a compelling proposition as its panels do not rely on polysilicon. As shown in the graphics above, China dominates polysilicon production, but for FSLR that’s not as big of a direct threat. That said, the Chinese government provides generous support to the Chinese solar industry, investing $130 billion USD in 2023, ultimately delivering cost advantages (which arguably may be unfair). The emerging glut of solar panels coming out of China has driven prices downward, with the IEA reporting that spot prices for solar panels in 2023 dropped by nearly 50%.

Crucially, many Chinese solar companies are heavily indebted, and the price wars in China and international markets should continue to add pressure on Chinese solar companies. At least 50 Chinese solar companies at various stages of the supply chain filed for bankruptcy in 2025. For example, according to Seeking Alpha's data, JinkoSolar’s (NYSE:JKS) current liabilities at nearly $7 billion, and total liabilities reach nearly $12.5 billion, a hefty amount for a company producing $11.4 billion in TTM revenues, down from $16.7 billion as of reporting in December 2023.

Chinese companies are moving to improve their situation, with industry players looking to set up a $7 billion fund to reduce polysilicon production by a third in an effort to prop up prices. Many Chinese solar companies have signed a “self-discipline” agreement, but it remains to be seen if such efforts will ultimately pay off. Difficulties in China, however, are good news for First Solar as it doesn't rely on Chinese inputs.

Putting America First With FirstSolar Solar Production

First Solar has been moving to ramp up domestic production facilities, launching plants in Alabama, Louisiana, and elsewhere. Former President Joe Biden championed the solar industry and provided extensive incentives to expand capacity. President Donald Trump isn’t likely to champion solar production specifically, but he has been working to revive America’s manufacturing sector as a whole.

First Solar’s CEO Mark R. Widmar believes that President Trump’s Big Beautiful Bill is going to be a major benefit for First Solar by keeping certain incentives in place, while also restricting access to Section 45X to companies relying on material assistance or products manufactured by foreign entities, including Chinese solar companies. Essentially, a loophole is being closed that would have benefited Chinese companies. Since First Solar doesn’t rely as extensively on Chinese inputs, they are better positioned in this current market.

Antidumping policies should also keep Chinese solar companies at bay, at least as far as the American market is concerned. The Department of Commerce has launched investigations into the Chinese solar industry, including efforts to circumvent market-protection policies through transhipment. The Big Beautiful Bill will phase out some renewable energy-specific incentives, however. Projects started by July 4th 2026 will still be eligible, which may lead to an increase in orders to get ahead of the deadline.

All in all, I am not as confident as First Solar’s CEO regarding the Big Beautiful Bill. As for the Trump administration, that too is a mixed bag. Efforts to spur American manufacturing and protect American markets from dumping should benefit First Solar. Deemphasizing solar may create headwinds, however. In the long run, I think there’s a fair chance that the Trump administration will warm up to solar, not in regards to renewable energy, but instead as a national security concern and a way to boost American manufacturing. This is speculation, however.

FSLR Fundamentals and Valuation

First Solar’s GAAP PE (TTM) is an attractive 15.79, versus a sector median of 28.1, and the non-GAAP PE measures about the same, versus a sector median of 22. The forward GAAP PE comes in at 12.2 versus a sector median of 30. Of course, PE ratios don’t tell us everything, but in an era of historically high PE ratios, I do find low PEs especially attractive. As for valuation, I turn to Alpha Sprea’s DCF calculator, which shows FSLR overvalued with modest assumptions.

I set the net income margin at 15% and the revenue growth rate at 10% to align with FSLR’s 5-year averages, according to Seeking Alpha’s data. During the last reporting, FSLR actually enjoyed a ~29% net income margin and ~15.4% revenue growth. I like to make conservative estimates; however, to try to get roughly an idea of where I think the short-term floor could be. As we can see, even with this conservative model, FSLR is undervalued by 14%. I set the discount rate at 10%, as I use that for all companies to make them easier to compare. The terminal growth rate is set at 4%, which aligns with the usually observed global GDP growth.

As for the balance sheet, FSLR has $1.153 billion cash and short-term investments. Cash and STI have been declining, which I presume is impacted by First Solar’s expansion of production facilities. Total assets, including plants and equipment, come in at ~$12.86 billion. Some plants are seeing production slashed in Vietnam and Malaysia, and I do worry they may not be as valuable now, amid the trade war, as they were last year. First Solar has a backlog worth 64.0 GW, which extends through 2030, which I find particularly appealing. Total liabilities are moderate at $4.311 billion.

It’s always nice when there’s unanimous agreement, and both my fellow Seeking Alpha analysts and the quant tools agree that FSLR is a buy right now. The quant grades, shown below, give FSLR high marks except for profitability and a mediocre revision grade. The low profitability marks draw my eye given that FSLR’s net income margin is quite healthy. Unfortunately, levered free cash flow growth is in the red by more than 32 percent, and the TTM return on asset turnover gets a D mark as well. Given that FSLR has been in a growth stage, expanding domestic and international operations, I’m not too worried about these factors, for now.

Why I Could Be Wrong

President Trump and the Republican Party are highly skeptical of renewable energies. While I believe ultimately that pursuing solar and especially a strong domestic solar industry that isn’t reliant on Chinese inputs, will put America first, those in Washington may disagree. The trade war may also cause disruptions, including retaliatory tariffs. Ultimately, First Solar will go as the domestic solar market goes, and any headwinds, political or otherwise, could weigh the company down. Further, while China's solar industry is suffering turmoil, I suspect in the long run the industry will emerge stronger as the price war cools off and subpar companies go under.

Key Takeaway: First Solar is a Buy

I believe solar energy can play a vital role in terms of reviving American manufacturing, improving and hardening the energy grid, and even for national defense. First Solar’s favorable valuation makes it attractive. That said, China’s dominance in the solar industry will create headwinds abroad, and domestic policy choices could disfavor First Solar, so there are risks investors should be aware of. Still, I think this is a moderate-risk investment that is justified by substantial upside, and thus FSLR is a buy.

data.title: High-Yield Closed-End Funds (Part 3): EHI
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

We continue to look at high-yield closed-end funds and get to know them in more detail. Today, we will examine Western Asset Global High Income Fund Inc. (NYSE:EHI).

Fund Overview

EHI was launched on 07/28/2003. At the end of the second quarter of 2025, the fund had total assets of around $ 289.77 million and total net assets of $205 million. The market capitalization of the fund is close to $200 million. The number of holdings is 403. The average maturity of the fund is 8.88 years. With an effective duration is 4.98 years, EHI is not overly sensitive.

The top three biggest investments in the portfolio are from Aercap Ireland CAP (1.25%), Venture Global LNG (1.23%), and First Quantum Minerals (1.12%). This suggests that the fund is betting on high-yielding but also high-risk sectors - aviation, energy, and mining.

The total return on NAV for three years is 4.56%, and for ten years it is 3.98%.

This CEF's distribution rate on NAV is 12.33%. EHI is paying its shareholders a $0.84 yearly dividend in the form of $0.0700 monthly distributions.

At the current moment, the market price of EHI is $6.60, with a NAV of $6.81 and a positive one-year Z-score of 0.53 and a positive 3-year Z-score of 0.44. It's currently trading at a 3.08% discount. It has a total leverage of 29.24%, an expense ratio of 4.14%, and a non-leverage expense ratio of 1.49%. This means that there are significant operating and leverage costs that must be accounted for by the investor.

And here is the pricing history for the last month:

The credit quality score of the fund is around a BB rating.

And here is the maturity breakdown, as we said above, it has an average maturity of 8.88 years:

EHI vs HYG

Here are the comparisons between EHI and iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG):

If we look at the 5-year total return comparison:

We can see that HYG outperforms EHI, but if we look at the:

3-year total return comparison:

Or 6-month total return comparison:

We see that they have gone hand in hand for a long time, and now we can see that there's a divergence, and EHI outperforms HYG.

Even if we leverage HYG, the 10-year total return still will be lower than the 10-year total return of EHI:

Conclusion

Despite its high expenses and lower credit rating, EHI is an interesting choice for investors seeking a stable monthly income with a conscious risk. Trading at a discount to NAV offers additional value, and the current yield above 12% is rare in the market. If interest rates remain stable or decline, the fund price could recover, and the yield could remain competitive. However, in an adverse economic scenario, credit risks could have a significant impact on the portfolio. EHI is not for everyone. It is an income-oriented fund with medium to high risk, suitable for investors with an appetite for high yield and a willingness to accept volatility and credit risk. It is suitable as part of a diversified portfolio, but not as a core position.

data.title: Chart Of The Day: Is The PPI Proof Of An Inflation Problem?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Everything was coming up roses on Wall Street and in Washington. Until Thursday morning at 8:30 am Eastern, that is. That’s when the Producer Price Index (PPI) inflation data dropped for the month of July.

Unlike earlier retail inflation figures, these wholesale inflation numbers were downright ugly. The headline reading rose 0.9% month-over-month, more than 4X the increase economists were expecting. The year-over-year inflation rate surged to 3.3% from 2.4% in June, as you can see in the MoneyShow Chart of the Day here.

(Source: Bureau of Labor Statistics)

What about the “core” number that excludes food and energy? It also jumped 0.9%, pushing core PPI up to 3.7% YOY. Other measures like “services less trade, transportation, and warehousing” or “goods less food and energy” all moved higher.

What does it all mean?

First, Wall Street will pay even closer attention to what Federal Reserve Chairman Jay Powell says next week at the Fed’s annual gathering in Jackson Hole, Wyoming. He’ll have a chance to “frame” the interest rate debate ahead of the Fed’s Sept. 16/17 policy meeting.

Second, markets still think the most likely outcome at that meeting is a 25-basis point rate cut. That would lower the federal funds rate to a range of 4-4.25%.

Third, Wall Street would love nothing more than for another pair of cuts to follow in October and December. Expectations for just such a scenario helped drive stocks higher earlier this week, including a huge two-day move in smaller-capitalization names.

But if this is the start of a series of reports suggesting greater pass-through effects from higher tariffs, that’s going to look more and more fanciful. And that could give both stock and bond markets agita. Keep that in mind with stocks trading in rarefied air.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
